speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but hold on. Yeah I don't remember-- How many-- do I only have one box? Yeah, one box out of 10. [INAUDIBLE]. But I'm also going to have the labels and leave the name blank. Because it'll be gone and we don't have to do this. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Would you like a drink? A Coke or something?

speaker: interviewer
text: I have-- I have some water, thank you. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four with Leon Wendel, tape four on April 24, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about how in the ghetto, by a certain time, everyone worked. Everyone had one of these jobs. What was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll get to it in a minute. So when-- everybody had to register. And I could not avoid anymore not working. From 1941, I went to work in-- in a metal factory. The metal factory was-- we were making nails. Those were nails for heavy boots-- like, mountain climbing nails. I mean, that the branch I was in-- there were other things for making wires and all that. And it was hard work. And my brother Leibel, he had some connections there. And connections in the ghetto was the difference between life and death. If you had connections and you could get places, then you could survive or you had a chance to survive. So he got me out of there. And he got me into the sanitation department. I became a janitor. A janitor meant that I have to get up every day and sweep up a certain amount of street. My street was on the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: On wait, hold on one sec.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to cut.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about being a janitor.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was a janitor till I was sent out. A few times-- we were selected a couple times. I wouldn't say a few. A couple times-- to be sent out of the ghetto all together. Like, resealing they called it. And my brother Leibel, having some connection, got us out of that. And none of those people who were ever sent out, whole families-- nobody ever survived. Whole families were destroyed. Now, being a janitor, my work wasn't hard. It-- I went up in the morning. I swept up the street. On the weekend, I had to take some-- some white wash and white wash the curb and the crossing line. And we had-- of course, you had to bury the-- the trash, the garbage. We had to dig a hole.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got together. It was groups, you know-- a section group. That group got together. Then they went someplace and dug a hole, throw in the trash. And next time, we went another place.

speaker: interviewee
text: There wasn't that much trash to begin with because there was nothing to throw away. So I worked for that. And when they got desperate to send out people, they picked whichever were not productive workers. And I was an unproductive worker. So they put me on the list to send out to work, to labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was in 1944. And I got the notification. By that time, there was no more in the ghetto than-- my father died in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. How did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- from hunger, malnutrition. And my oldest brother died in ghetto. He got cancer on the brain. He was in hospital maybe for four weeks. He got weaker and weaker. They couldn't do nothing. There was nothing-- they can't even do nothing today I don't think, if I think back about it. And he died. But my father died from hunger, from malnutrition, from-- and then they sent me a paper that I have to report to the jail or the-- we called it a jail, you know, the gathering point-- to be sent out for labor. By that time in 1944, people did not go voluntarily, not even '43 or '42. People-- because I remember the trains came back. And the trains brought back clothing. And a cousin of mine by the name [INAUDIBLE] worked at that clothing. And he said some of the clothing had holes in it, like bullet holes. And they had baby clothing. And it came to our house. And his wife and two children were already sent away. And he came in-- of course, this take also a long story-- and he came to my mother. And he said, Chana, I think they killed those children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother said-- and you told me a way that I couldn't listen. So I wouldn't listen. But I heard it anyway, you know. She didn't want me to listen. How can you say a thing like that? You crazy? I mean, kill children? They kill children? It's impossible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, he was one who believed it. But 99 and 1/9 of a percent did not believe that it's even possible. So when they came to send me out, there was left me, my sister, and my mother. So I didn't want to go. We didn't want to let me go. So I was starting to hide out. So first, I went to sleep at my cousin's. Then they announced that if the people are not gonna get-- show up, they will arrest wherever they find them, including the people who hide them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one time, I was at home in daytime. Usually, they came at night. I was home in daytime. And they came and they arrested me. Going out from the house on the street, one arrested me, one-- one policeman. He didn't have a gun or anything. You know. So I ran. He couldn't-- I was still younger than him. He couldn't catch me. I guess he didn't even make too much of an effort, you know? So I was hiding out like this, sleeping here and sleeping there. And people got afraid to hold you. And they made the-- we called it a [NON-ENGLISH] you know, like a sweep.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took a whole block of houses, and they went through the houses and see if they can get anybody who didn't have-- who didn't have the-- the ration card. Didn't have the ration card, then you were-- you were wanted. The first thing they did when they sent me the paper, they cut off my ration. I could not get the ration anymore. So I was hiding out like this for three weeks, almost four weeks. And we were so hungry-- there was only two rations for three. And-- and the ration wasn't enough at all to begin with. So when they-- when they did arrest me, I was glad that I was arrested. I was happy about it. You know. I didn't go myself. But I was at peace. So they took me down to this camp or jail, whatever-- this gathering place. And my mother came down. And we spoke through the fence. You know. And they said that the transport is going to go this day, that day. It dragged on. I was there a few days. And she took off the ring, her wedding ring. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she gave it to me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was the last I saw her. I have to compose myself. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then on March the 4th in the morning, they took us out. And they sent us, and they loaded on trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a German came and made a speech that we were going to Cieszanow. Now, why the people believe that, I don't-- think back now, you know? Anyway, we did go to Cieszanow.

speaker: interviewee
text: It took us all day to get there. I mean, it's-- it's an hour's drive by regular train, you know, two hour's drive. Anyway, it doesn't matter. We went-- we came to Cieszanow. And we were 1,700 people on the train. And we came there. They let us down. And of course, I didn't know what was happening in back of the train. And when they came to the camp where they took us, we were 580 people. And the rest-- later, we found out the rest went also to Cieszanow, but in a different location, in a different camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What we came to was a-- was a big factory. It was called [INAUDIBLE]. It was a French-owned factory before the war. It was making cloth or-- anyway, clothes. Cotton or that-- what is it? You know, fancy, more expensive. And we were [? hold ?] there. And a big barrack was prepared.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were three layers of-- of bunks all along, not separated. We were, you know-- let's say the barrack was 50 feet long. It was a 50 foot long bunk. The barrack was much longer than that. And we got some food, more than in the ghetto-- much better. And we sat there. After about three days in those barracks-- they let us out in day time-- they said the factory is not finished. The factory is being built. And in the meantime, they would take us out and divide us. And we would help the Polish labor build the factory. So-- not all of us. There wasn't enough work for all of us. But I got out to work. And they wanted some volunteers. Of course, nobody wanted to volunteer. There's no volunteers. So they took about 20 people. And they worked, and they came back, and they didn't say nothing, you know. So next day, they wanted volunteers. And those went voluntarily.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the rest said, oh, there's got to be something good if they want-- if they want back, they want to go. And what was the good about it? We came in contact with the Poles.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Poles had unlimited bread. There was no rationing of bread. And for a handkerchief, you could get a bread or maybe more. They had no clothing of any kind. The Jewish people had clothing. We-- by nature, we had a lot of clothing. In the house, even at the poorest house, there was clothing for the people that [? require it. ?] So if you could-- and when we left the ghetto to go to this camp, this was not a concentration camp. This was a [GERMAN], a working camp. This came into-- into-- it was militarily administrated, but it was not SS or anything like that. It wasn't the concentration camp. So when the Poles started bread, I had a new suit from my brother, which I took along. He was not alive anymore. And I sold that suit for maybe 1,500 zlot. Money. I took money. We already knew that you can buy with money, with their money. And every day, I would buy something. You know. Not that-- I don't think-- maybe I did get to use that. So we had no problem. I have plenty of food, plenty of dry food, plenty of bread. We even-- there came times that we didn't even care to take the soup that the kitchen was making for us because we had better things we bought from the Poles with clothes. And we stayed, and we worked to finish the factory. And we got it finished. And they started bringing in the machines, having machinery. I don't know what the machines were. And the factory was built to receive those machines. Because ground was laid out that machines fit into the cement. It was so built modern. And at night, we started to hear guns, rumbling. And the people who-- who watched us were the Russian-- Vlasov's army, it was called. They were Russian or Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they put up watchtowers to look out instead of in. We were in. Because they were afraid of the partisans, which were-- what would you call partisans, you know? They were fighting against the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we heard rumblings. And then one day, they-- the machines what they brought, they took them back. And when you're late at night, you could hear the rumbling going all night. And we knew from the Poles that the war is close, that the Russians are-- that the Russians are close. They were already by Warsaw. And it's not far from Warsaw. I don't know exactly how many miles Cieszanow from Warsaw is, but not too far. And it came the order to ship-- to ship us out of Cieszanow.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was in August, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the ghetto, I had one letter from my mother, a postcard saying that my sister was sent out to work, which I know now that it wasn't work. She was sent right there to Chelmno. She never survived. And my mother is alone in ghetto. And the letter said, the card said, she was sent to a town in Germany which was fictitious. The town existed, but was fictitious. Augsburg, the city of Augsburg-- but they never went to Augsburg. They never went more than 20 miles behind lodge, where they were killed. And then he took us with a train-- they put us on a train and they sent us into Germany, supposedly to the same factory with the Poles to work in Cieszanow. The day or two days before, the factory-- we were sent out. The SS came and took over. And it was already a different story. Before, you could still talk to a German [? or ?] [? think-- ?] But we still didn't realize what it was. And they put us on a train. And we traveled to Buchenwald. It took us maybe four days at least. The train consisted of a big cattle wagon, which was divided in three parts. And the two sides were people from-- us, the prisoners. And in the middle was divided, was a soldier sitting there. I don't remember. Or every night or some nights, the soldier went down, and they locked the wagons. We were given a little water. And if we were stopped someplace on the sidewalk, on a sideline, which was plenty, they did allow us to go down and do toilet or things like this. And we arrived in Buchenwald. I don't remember the exact date. But it was late in August or very beginning of September. A couple days before we arrived, they-- Buchenwald had a factory working for the German armament. I think the name was Gustloff-- the Gustloff Werke. And they bombed-- the Americans bombed the factory. And the line that they-- line got burned out. The delousing barracks, which means you weren't-- disaffecting barracks and the shower barracks, were also burned out. So when we came, and we came at night, it looked like a [NON-ENGLISH], like hell. We came in. And he-- they drove us on that line in. And here was coal. There was coal for the camp. Maybe-- I don't know how many hundreds of yards stacked up with coal. And the whole pile was burning, like a big fire. And there were burned out wagons. And they took us down, and they led us into a barrack. And the people who came to take us down-- was all at night-- to take us down wearing the striped suits. And they had dogs, big dogs. And those were kapos. I didn't know that. And they controlled us. You know, we're 580 people. It was hard to control. We were thirsty and hungry and tired and all that. This was the middle of the night. And here was this square chimney belching fire-- not smoke, but fire. And the smell-- it was a smell. This was the crematorium. We didn't know that. And the smell was terrible. And in the morning, we saw already. So they made us undress. And we stood outside. We undressed outside. We put all-- all our clothing in a package and laid it down. I took my mother's ring, and I put it in a piece of soap. I squeezed it in and I took it with me. Of course, this was silly too, because if not for the that fire I would have never seen the soap or the clothing. Now, what happens is that the fire burned their store rooms. And they had no striped suits to give us out. So a guy came. There was no water in the camp, no water, period. A guy came. And they had a water hose. First, they shaved us, cut off the hair with their machines. Then they had this big tank. And we walked over. And it was a case. We were standing in a line. And they told you to get into that tank. It was like a-- like a big bowl, like a small jacuzzi or something like that. And they grabbed you on the side, and they dipped you in it. And this was Lysol, a mix of Lysol. And when I got out, this was burning like fire. They told you close your eyes. Because they just cut my hair and cut my hair. And I imagine the machines they use weren't the best. You were nicked. And it burned like fire and there was no water. And then they-- somebody came with a big hose. And they sprayed us.

speaker: interviewer
text: 580 people-- can you imagine being sprayed with a hose, with one hose? And you tried to get there, to catch a little of that hose, which I don't think I even made it. And then they-- we went back and took that clothing which we-- which we brought in the first place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this was a lucky streak. Because before they sent us out from-- from Cieszanow, they took us to select clothing at an army base and-- and-- and divide it and things. And I went in that place. And there was clothing piled to the ceiling from prisoners, from Russian prisoners, all kinds of prisoners-- maybe American, English. I picked out the best [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], what they call undershirts, everything. I picked out a pair of shoes, you understand, instead of having those wooden clogs. Not just I-- all-- all from-- all people from that camp. And I took along a lot of clothes. So standing out there-- and we were standing there. And we looked. And we're not in the camp yet. We were in the camp, but there was still a fence to go into the proper camp. And I see a guy. And I recognize him. And I yell over, [NON-ENGLISH]. And he says, yeah, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Was does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That my brother's here. So I knew he was there. And we stayed there. And he came running.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but he couldn't get to me-- just saw each other. And then we were put into a barracks. The barracks was a long barracks with two uplinks from both sides. With three layers of-- you have seen the pictures from Buchenwald-- the Buchenwald barracks, you know. [NON-ENGLISH], you know, the sign when you go in and all that. And we stayed in that barrack, the whole group, the whole 560, and maybe a lot more from others in the same barrack. And we got our soup 5:00 in the morning, and-- and we got the tea which was made from a-- from a leaf. It's used today too, it's used. Anyway, it-- it-- I washed in it instead of drinking it. You know. We didn't have any water. And maybe for five days till the water was reinstated in the camp, water was rationed. You got a glass or two of water a day. Now, I was in Buchenwald. And while I was in Buchenwald, I saw my brother every day. We could-- within that camp we were-- we could see each other in-- in the camp, the camps. But we happened to be--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your brother Manny.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my brother Manny. So I gave him some of the clothing I had because he was already in the striped paper suit which was made out of paper. And I had some barn clothes I gave to him. And we were together, I don't know, maybe four weeks in that camp or something. We could not get into the single. They kept our group together. Supposedly, we were already trained people, which is not true. We were not trained. And we go into the same factory, which we did. Excuse me. And then after-- we got some shots there, which I don't know. There were days which they gave me a shot every day. In this breast and in this breast-- every day. The barrack was closed at night. And before they let you out, anybody who wanted to go out had to get a shot. So I don't know how many shots. What the shots were, I don't know-- no idea. We had x-rays. He had-- we went to a doctor. After they installed the water, we could go and take a shower. There were-- I mean, real showers. In Buchenwald, there was no gas. People were shot. Every day in the morning, there was an appel in that big, giant place. And at that time, we-- it was about 30,000 people there. And if people were missing, then they were looking for them. We were counted every day. So you had to bring out the dead and lay them down to be counted. People died laying down. So sometimes, you could stay for hours till the figure was right. And after that, they-- they sent us out of there as a group. My brother went away before me with his group. But the group he came, they were sent away, and I don't know where. And this was already in September '44. From there, we were sent away into a-- a working camp, to Sonneberg-- Sonneberg and [INAUDIBLE], which is very close to Weimar, the city of Weimar. And it was this big factory, GE Reinhardt, if I remember going back. [INAUDIBLE] they were making gears-- gears for tanks or airplanes or whatever. And I came in there. They took us down and they set us up and then made a big line. And that chef came down, a chef that-- the chef, he would call them here there director or whatever, you know. And he made a big speech. It's all your fault. The Juden wanted the war. The Juden are to blame and now you've going to work and you're going to this and that-- blew a lot of-- this were supposed to be the pep talk-- blew a lot of air. OK, we didn't pay much attention to him anyway. Doesn't matter. Then they come out and ask somebody who has any kind of mechanical experience. So I said I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went out and asked me what can you work on. I said, I can work in a drill press, a punch press. So they put me on a machine. It was a long machine, maybe 10 meter long.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it had this giant roll in the middle. And you put down this big wheel which weighed about at least 50 pounds. You secured it. You put the pin through. It was very hard work. Secured it from the other side and you had a handle, and you turned on the machine. And the machine pulled that giant roll through the hole of that gear. And that roll had teeth. And it cut gears inside the wheel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're gonna change to a new tape. OK.
